title: Before Independence: Văn Lang, Âu Lạc, a Thousand Years of China
bullets: The Đông Sơn culture cast spectacular bronze drums before any Chinese arrived, the signature artifact of a settled rice-farming society that became, two thousand years later, the emblem of a national identity older than the conquest.

Vietnamese tradition fills the silence with the Hùng kings of Văn Lang ruling for an impossible two and a half millennia — national origin myth, not history, written down a thousand years after the supposed events.
An Dương Vương's Âu Lạc left something you can stand inside: the great spiral earthwork ramparts of Cổ Loa near modern Hanoi, the oldest large fortification in Vietnam.
In 111 BCE the Han dynasty under Emperor Wu destroyed Nanyue and annexed the Red River delta, beginning the thousand years Vietnamese tradition calls Bắc thuộc, the Northern Domination.
China installed an entire operating system: the chữ Hán writing system, Confucian bureaucracy, and Mahayana Buddhism, arriving by sea at the port of Luy Lâu around the second century.

Vietnam did not resist China by staying primitive — it learned the conqueror's whole civilization and used it to stay permanently not-China, the opposite mechanism from the Khmer absorbing India voluntarily.
The first resistance heroes are women: the Trưng Sisters expelled the Han in 40 CE for three years, and Lady Triệu rose in 248 — real events, heavily mythologized by much later writers.

After eight centuries of failed revolts the Tang dynasty simply collapsed, and around 905 the Khúc family took over the administration as autonomous governors — independence by China letting go, not yet by victory.

title: Bạch Đằng 938 and the Ngô–Đinh–Early Lê Founding
bullets: In 938 Ngô Quyền planted iron-capped stakes hidden under the high tide in the Bạch Đằng river, lured the Southern Han fleet over them, and let the falling tide impale and destroy the ships.
Ngô Quyền proclaimed himself king in 939, ending a thousand years of Chinese rule; Vietnamese historiography treats 938 as the true birth of the nation.
The tidal-stake stratagem becomes a recurring national symbol — a Vietnamese commander does the exact same thing to the Mongols on the same river 350 years later.

Ngô Quyền died in 944 and the state collapsed into the Anarchy of the Twelve Warlords — winning independence and building a state are completely different problems, and Vietnam nearly failed the second within a decade.
Đinh Bộ Lĩnh reunified the country in 968, named the state Đại Cồ Việt, and called himself emperor — asserting Vietnam was a parallel empire equal to China, not a piece of it.
The double register of emperor at home and tributary abroad is the diplomatic machine Vietnam ran for the next nine hundred years.
The Đinh ruled from Hoa Lư, a defensible capital tucked among limestone karst mountains, chosen because a young state surrounded by enemies needed walls of stone it had not had to build.
Lê Hoàn founded the Early Lê dynasty in 980 and defeated a Song invasion in 981 — again at Bạch Đằng — confirming independence by force against the reunified Chinese empire itself.

title: The Lý Dynasty: Thăng Long and a Buddhist State
bullets: Lý Công Uẩn took the throne in 1009 as Lý Thái Tổ, founding the Lý dynasty — Vietnam's first long, stable, institution-building dynasty, lasting over two centuries.
In 1010 he moved the capital from the mountain fortress of Hoa Lư to the open Red River plain at Thăng Long, modern Hanoi — a state secure enough to govern, not cower.
The One Pillar Pagoda, a wooden lotus rising on a single stone pillar from a pond, expressed the Lý state's Buddhist soul and is still a national emblem.

The Lý built the Temple of Literature in 1070, the Imperial Academy in 1076, and held the first civil service examination in 1075 — three centuries before any European university existed in the region.
The Temple of Literature was the seed of the Confucian scholar-bureaucracy that would, four centuries later, quietly conquer the state and dismantle the Buddhism that planted it.
The Lý state was Buddhist to its core, patronizing Thiền Zen lineages and giving monasteries land and tax exemption, with monks serving as its first ministers.
Large-scale Red River dike and embankment systems tied legitimacy to water: the dynasty that kept the dikes kept the harvest, and the dynasty that kept the harvest kept the throne.
The Lý ended in 1225 the usual Vietnamese way — not conquered from outside but hollowed within, the throne passing to the Trần clan through a marriage and a coup.

title: The Lý–Song War and the Cham Frontier
bullets: Rather than wait for the Song to invade, the eunuch-general Lý Thường Kiệt struck first in 1075, leading an army and fleet into China and storming the Song city of Yongzhou.
When the Song counter-invaded in 1077 Lý Thường Kiệt held them on the Như Nguyệt River line, fought them to a standstill, and let them negotiate withdrawal rather than humiliating the empire.
The poem Nam quốc sơn hà, declaring the Southern country belongs to the Southern emperor by Heaven's decree, is routinely called Vietnam's first declaration of independence.
The Cham spoke an Austronesian language, the seafaring island family of Malay and Polynesian, utterly unrelated to Austroasiatic Vietnamese — as far apart as English and a Malay sailor.
Champa was a maritime people on the coast, Đại Việt a riverine-agrarian people of the delta; their absorption is the swallowing of one distinct civilization by another, not a nation reuniting.
This is the same Cham who sacked Angkor in 1177; the mainland was a three-cornered fight, and Suryavarman II of Angkor invaded Đại Việt in the 1120s–40s as part of it.
In 1149 the Lý opened the island port of Vân Đồn, Đại Việt's first major international trading station, plugging the kingdom into the maritime commerce that linked Song China and the Srivijaya world.
The Lý ended in 1225 with the throne passing to the Trần clan through a marriage and a coup engineered by the clan strongman Trần Thủ Độ.

title: The Trần Dynasty and the Three Mongol Invasions
bullets: The Trần came to power in 1225 by marrying the seven-year-old empress Lý Chiêu Hoàng to a Trần boy, then securing it by exterminating the Lý clan — a marriage of children and a massacre of cousins.
The Trần institutionalized the retired-emperor system: an emperor stepped back to train his successor, producing smooth successions in a culture where succession was usually lethal.
The Mongols invaded Đại Việt three times — 1258, 1285, 1287–88 — and Đại Việt defeated them three times, one of the great military records of the medieval world.
The template all three times: evacuate Thăng Long, withdraw food and people, let the climate and disease wear the invaders down, then counter-attack the weakened army.
Facing the huge 1285 pincer, the retired emperor convened the Diên Hồng conference and asked the elders to fight or submit; their roared answer became the founding myth of Vietnamese popular resolve.
Trần Hưng Đạo's Hịch tướng sĩ, the Proclamation to the Officers, shamed the comfortable and invoked duty and rage before the war — one of the most famous texts in the language.
In 1285 Trần Hưng Đạo's counter-offensives at Hàm Tử and Chương Dương shattered the Mongol pincer and drove both arms out.
Lê Văn Hưu's Đại Việt sử ký of 1272 was the first national history, and scholars adapted Chinese characters into the chữ Nôm script to write spoken Vietnamese — resisting China with Chinese tools.

title: Trần Hưng Đạo, the Bạch Đằng Stakes, and the High Trần
bullets: Kublai Khan's third invasion in 1287 came with a large seaborne supply fleet so the army would not starve; Trần Hưng Đạo went after the fleet instead.

At Bạch Đằng in 1288 he reenacted Ngô Quyền's 938 trick at colossal scale — stakes, the tide, the whole Yuan fleet annihilated as the water dropped — the most celebrated victory in Vietnamese history.

That a commander defeated the Mongols by consciously reenacting the founding battle of independence is the central proof-text of the national idea: one continuous people who win the same way, forever.

Trần Hưng Đạo wrote his strategy down and, after his death in 1300, became a god — worshipped as Đức Thánh Trần, a rare case of a documented general with a datable battle record becoming a popular deity.
Trần Nhân Tông, who led the realm through two Mongol wars, abdicated to become a monk and founded the Trúc Lâm school, the first distinctively Vietnamese Buddhist lineage.
The Cham king Chế Bồng Nga mounted Champa's last great resurgence, sacking Thăng Long itself in 1371 and again in 1377 and 1383; a Vietnamese king died invading Vijaya in 1377.
Chế Bồng Nga was killed by gunfire in a river battle in 1390, and with him died Champa's ability to threaten the north.
The late Trần was wrecked by Cham humiliations, fiscal crisis, and a rising class of Confucian scholars hostile to the Buddhist monasteries — into the vacuum stepped the reformist official Hồ Quý Ly.

title: The Ming Occupation and Lê Lợi's War of Liberation
bullets: Hồ Quý Ly deposed the last Trần in 1400 and rammed through a startlingly modern reform package — early state paper money, land and slave caps, expanded exams, the chữ Nôm script.
His Citadel of the Hồ Dynasty, a colossal dressed-stone fortress raised in months, still stands as a UNESCO site — the great monument of a regime that barely outlived its mortar.
In 1406 the Ming under the Yongle emperor invaded on the pretext of restoring the Trần, captured the Hồ emperors, and annexed the country outright as the province of Jiaozhi.

For twenty years Đại Việt was direct Chinese territory again — and the Ming deliberately burned, confiscated, or shipped north the Vietnamese written record, the reason the earlier history is so thin.
The landowner Lê Lợi raised the Lam Sơn uprising in 1418, a near-hopeless guerrilla insurgency that survived years of almost-annihilation in the mountains.
The scholar-strategist Nguyễn Trãi waged a coordinated war of military maneuver and political persuasion, an early model of integrated insurgency.
Lê Lợi annihilated the Ming relief army at the Chi Lăng pass in 1427, stranding the occupation force with no hope of reinforcement.
Nguyễn Trãi's Bình Ngô đại cáo of 1428 announced victory and independence in ringing classical prose — Vietnam's second declaration of independence — and Lê Lợi founded the Later Lê dynasty.

title: Lê Thánh Tông and the Confucian Golden Age
bullets: Nguyễn Trãi, the hero of the liberation, was executed with three generations of his family over the Lệ Chi Viên affair — the most famous miscarriage of justice in Vietnamese history.
Lê Thánh Tông's 37-year reign is the conventional golden age, a reign of systems: law, administration, cartography, and literature reorganized at once.
He reorganized the country into thirteen regular provinces run by examination-recruited officials, abolishing the old aristocratic commands — a centralized bureaucratic state on the Ming model.
The Hồng Đức Code granted women real property and inheritance rights, unusually protective by the standards of contemporary East Asian law — protections the later Nguyễn code would roll back.
Ngô Sĩ Liên compiled the Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư, the Complete Annals, still the single most important narrative source for the entire pre-modern past.
The Confucian scholar-bureaucracy decisively displaced Buddhism from the state; the men who ran Vietnam were now mandarins formed in the Temple of Literature, not monks.
In 1471 Lê Thánh Tông stormed and obliterated the Cham capital Vijaya, killed or deported tens of thousands, and reduced Champa to a small client rump — the great leap of the nam tiến.
After Lê Thánh Tông the dynasty declined fast — what happens when a perfected bureaucratic state loses the one thing it cannot bureaucratize, a competent man at the top.

title: The Mạc Usurpation and the Lê Restoration
bullets: After Lê Thánh Tông the throne passed through weak, short-lived, and murderous reigns, and within a single generation the perfected Lê state was hollow.
In 1527 the general Mạc Đăng Dung deposed the Lê and founded the Mạc dynasty, triggering sixty-five years of civil war over who was the legitimate emperor.
The Mạc even formally submitted to Ming China in 1540, ceding border land — a humiliation Lê propaganda never let anyone forget.
Loyalists proclaimed a Lê prince emperor in 1533, founding the Revival Lê — restored in name but a puppet of the warlords who fought for him from the very first day.
Trịnh Kiểm took over the Lê-loyalist cause, founding the line of Trịnh lords who would rule the north behind the Lê throne for two centuries.
In 1558 Nguyễn Hoàng, distrusted by his Trịnh in-laws, got the governorship of remote Thuận Hóa and quietly built an autonomous power base — the seed of the partition and of southern Vietnam.
Trịnh Tùng captured Thăng Long and broke the main Mạc line in 1592, reuniting the north under a restored Lê emperor who was in reality his prisoner.
From the Restoration on, the Lê emperor reigned but did not rule — a legitimating relic kept on the throne while the warlords held real power, one of history's most durable political fictions.

title: The Trịnh–Nguyễn Partition: Two Vietnams
bullets: For roughly two centuries there were two Vietnams: not a country at war with a breakaway region, but two fully formed states ruled in the name of the same powerless Lê emperor.
The north, Đàng Ngoài, known to Europeans as Tonkin, was ruled from Thăng Long by the Trịnh lords through a parallel chancellery behind the puppet emperor.
The south, Đàng Trong or Cochinchina, was ruled from the Huế region by the Nguyễn lords, technically loyal subjects of the same emperor while functioning as an independent state.
Seven northern offensives in the Trịnh–Nguyễn War between 1627 and 1672 failed to break the south, which held behind massive earthen ramparts around Đồng Hới.
Both sides reached for European firepower — the Dutch East India Company allied with the Trịnh, the Portuguese sold the Nguyễn cannon — the civil war fought partly with European artillery.
Jesuit missionaries systematized by Alexandre de Rhodes, whose dictionary was printed in 1651, devised the Latin-alphabet chữ Quốc ngữ — now the everyday script of a hundred million people.
Cut off from the north, the Nguyễn south threw open the port of Hội An, one of Southeast Asia's great harbors, with permanent Japanese and Chinese quarters.
After 1672 the two sides simply stopped along a fortified line near the Gianh River — no treaty, no winner, just two countries that had given up on being one.

title: The Nam Tiến: the Southward March and the End of Champa
bullets: Over eight centuries the Vietnamese center of gravity migrated about 800 miles from the Red River delta to the Mekong — the nam tiến, the single largest fact in the shape of modern Vietnam.
Modern Vietnam's strange shape — a thin 1,000-mile ribbon of coast, fat at both ends, pinched in the middle — is a fossil of this migration.
The first great surge was 1471: Lê Thánh Tông's destruction of Vijaya ended Champa as a power and pushed the border far south in one stroke.
The Cham spoke an Austronesian language unrelated to Austroasiatic Vietnamese; the nam tiến was not a nation reuniting but one distinct people conquering and absorbing another.
Champa survived after 1471 only as the small principality of Panduranga, whittled away by the Nguyễn lords, the last Cham polity formally extinguished in 1832.
In 1623 a Nguyễn lord obtained Cambodian permission to settle and tax Prey Nokor; within decades it was Vietnamese Sài Gòn, the hinge of the Mekong frontier.
Ming loyalist refugees fleeing the Manchu conquest were resettled into the lower Mekong in 1679 — Chinese refugees deployed as agents of Vietnamese expansion.
By 1757 a Cambodia squeezed between Siam and Vietnam had ceded the entire lower Mekong delta to the Nguyễn — the land that fed Angkor became southern Vietnam.

title: The Tây Sơn Rebellion
bullets: The Tây Sơn rebellion is the earthquake of Vietnamese history, destroying in turn the Nguyễn lords, the Trịnh lords, and the Lê dynasty itself, then collapsing almost as fast as it rose.
Almost the entire surviving record was written by the dynasty that destroyed them; modern Vietnam reveres them as national heroes, and the truth is argued over to this day.
Three brothers from Tây Sơn village raised a tax revolt in 1771 that swelled into the largest popular upheaval in Vietnamese history; by 1777 they had killed almost the whole Nguyễn lordly family.
In 1785 Nguyễn Huệ annihilated a Siamese fleet and army at Rạch Gầm–Xoài Mút, one of the most decisive battles in Vietnamese history.
In 1786 he marched north, smashed the Trịnh, and ended two centuries of Trịnh rule in weeks; the Lê dynasty then disintegrated and its last emperor fled to China in 1789.
Nguyễn Huệ proclaimed himself Emperor Quang Trung — a commoner-general crowning himself, the precise opposite of the dynastic principle.
In the Tết days of 1789 Quang Trung destroyed the occupying Qing army at Ngọc Hồi–Đống Đa and retook the capital in days — the modern-era Bạch Đằng.
Quang Trung died suddenly at about forty in 1792, and the Tây Sơn unravelled within a decade while the survivor Nguyễn Ánh rebuilt in the south with French help from Pigneau de Béhaine.

title: Gia Long, the Nguyễn Founding, and the Name "Vietnam"
bullets: In 1802 Nguyễn Ánh defeated the last Tây Sơn forces and proclaimed himself Emperor Gia Long, founder of the Nguyễn dynasty, the last dynasty of Vietnam.
For the first time one government ruled continuously from the Chinese border to the tip of Cà Mau — the entire ribbon the nam tiến had assembled over eight centuries.
Gia Long ruled from a new imperial capital at Huế in the center, deliberately placed to balance the two halves of the stretched country.
The Qing granted the inverted form Việt Nam, made official in 1804 — after a thousand years of other names, the country had the name it still carries.
This narrative ends here because 1804 is not a battle but a naming: the moment Đại Việt becomes Việt Nam, closing the arc that began with stakes in the Bạch Đằng river in 938.
The Nguyễn turned hard and conservative, adopting a Qing-modeled legal code that rolled back the Lê-era legal protections for women in favor of strict Confucian orthodoxy.
Gia Long built his Huế citadel on a French Vauban-style star-fort plan executed by Vietnamese labor — the two civilizations fused in one wall.
The French officers and arms that helped Gia Long win, and the Catholic communities his successors persecuted, became the thread the French would pull to conquer Vietnam within sixty years.
